questions.question: Define the critical section problem and state its three essential requirements.
keyPoints: Critical section is the code segment accessing shared resources
Three requirements: Mutual exclusion (only one process in CS), Progress (selection cannot be postponed), Bounded waiting (limits entry attempts after request)

questions.question: Explain the difference between progress and bounded waiting requirements in critical section solutions.
keyPoints: Progress: If no process in CS and some want to enter, only processes not in remainder can decide; cannot postpone indefinitely
Bounded waiting: After process requests entry, there is a limit on how many times others can enter before it gets admitted
Progress ensures no deadlock; bounded waiting prevents starvation

questions.question: What is a race condition? How does it relate to the critical section problem?
keyPoints: Race condition occurs when behavior depends on relative timing of concurrent processes
In critical section problem, race conditions occur when multiple processes access shared resources without proper synchronization
Results in unpredictable/incorrect output due to interleaving of operations

questions.question: Explain the TestAndSet instruction and how it can be used to solve the critical section problem.
keyPoints: TestAndSet atomically reads and sets a boolean variable to true in one operation
Returns old value, allowing process to check if lock was available
Can implement mutual exclusion by using a shared lock variable; process enters CS if TestAndSet returns false

questions.question: What is busy-waiting? Discuss its advantages and disadvantages.
keyPoints: Busy-waiting (spinlock) is when a process repeatedly checks a condition in a loop while waiting
Advantage: Simple to implement, no context switching overhead for short waits
Disadvantage: Wastes CPU cycles, inefficient for long wait times

questions.question: Describe the structure of a process solution pattern showing Entry, Critical, Exit, and Remainder sections.
keyPoints: Entry section: Request permission to enter CS, contains synchronization code
Critical section: Code accessing shared resources requiring protection
Exit section: Signals departure from CS, allows other processes to enter
Remainder section: Code with no request to enter CS, executes after exit

questions.question: What is starvation in the context of the critical section problem? How is it prevented?
keyPoints: Starvation occurs when a process perpetually waits to enter CS because others keep entering first
Caused by violating bounded waiting requirement
Prevented by ensuring bounded waiting: limiting number of entries by other processes after a request

questions.question: Explain Peterson's solution for two processes. Show how it satisfies all three requirements of the critical section problem.
keyPoints: Uses shared variables 'turn' and 'flag[2]'; Process sets flag to true and turn to other process
Entry section: While other wants to enter and it's other's turn, wait
Mutual exclusion satisfied: Both cannot be in CS simultaneously
Progress satisfied: If other not wanting, can proceed; only those in entry/exit compete
Bounded waiting satisfied: After P0 indicates interest, P1 can enter at most once before P0

questions.question: Compare hardware-based solutions (TestAndSet and Swap) with software-based solutions (like Peterson's) for the critical section problem.
keyPoints: Hardware solutions: Use atomic CPU instructions; simpler to implement; often use busy-waiting
Software solutions: Use only shared variables; no special hardware needed; more complex to design
TestAndSet: Atomically sets lock and returns old value; efficient for single lock
Swap: Exchanges values of two variables; can create multiple locks
Trade-offs: Hardware solutions are more efficient but require special instructions; software solutions are portable

questions.question: Discuss the bakery algorithm for N processes. Explain how it achieves mutual exclusion, progress, and bounded waiting.
keyPoints: Based on ticket-numbering like customers at bakery; each process gets a number
Process with lowest number enters CS; if same number, process with lower ID enters
Mutual exclusion: Only one process can have lowest number at a time
Progress: Non-zero processes can get numbers and enter
Bounded waiting: After getting number, process waits at most N-1 others before entering

questions.question: What is atomicity? Why is it important in solving the critical section problem? Give examples of atomic operations.
keyPoints: Atomicity: Property of operation executing as single indivisible unit without interruption
Critical for CS solutions because non-atomic operations can be interrupted, leading to race conditions
Examples: TestAndSet instruction, Swap instruction, hardware-fetched instructions
Without atomicity, checking lock and setting it could be interleaved by another process
Operating systems provide atomic operations through hardware support
